channel.title: API on Dusty Turner
channel.description: Recent content in API on Dusty Turner

item.title: Fitbit and R Part 1: Accessing Data with the Fitbit API
item.description:
Introduction

In part 1 of this series, I will demonstrate how to interact with the Fitbit API in R.

Huge credit to Matt Kaye for creating the fitbitr package that provides an interface between R and the Fitbit API. If you are familiar with R and you have a Fitbit, you should have success following what I have laid out below.

Install the fitbitr package

From CRAN:

install.packages("fitbitr")

Or the development version from github:

item.title: The Peloton API Part 2: A Peloton Data Tribute to my Wife
item.description:
Peloton R

In part 1 of my Peloton API post, I explored the pelotonR created by Ben Weiher’s. It’s a great package for accessing your Peloton data and I recommend it.

In part 2, I’ll explore a different pelotonR package from Laura Ellis. She provides a great tutorial to display the functionality. I recommend this one too!

Objectives

The objectives for this post are to…

  1. Show how awesome my wife is.
  2. Highlight the functionality of Laura’s pelotonR package.
  3. Create visualizations from my wife and my Peloton data to show how awesome she is.

Speaking of visualizations, here’s another picture of my wife crushing a Peloton ride

item.title: ESPN Fantasy Football 2020 Part 1: Accessing the API
item.description:
Its that time of year!

Its that time of year again. The time when we dust off the old ESPN fantasy football API R code and fix everything that broke in the last year.

Here’s what I hope to show over the next few posts.

  1. How to access your ESPN public fantasy football league’s data.
  2. How to organize that data and create a few interesting displays.
  3. How to create a dashboard to supplement your league’s fun.

My ultimate goal is to build this into a package on Github. I’ve got that started, but its currently a work in progress.

item.title: The Peloton API Part 1: Accessing and Exploring
item.description:
In 2018, my wife and I were fortunate enough to find a deeply discounted Peloton on an online garage sale. Since then, she’s been going hard and I have been trying to keep up when the weather is bad or injuries keep me from running.

Over the last few months, a few works projects have taught me a lot about purrr, functional programming, collaborative projects, and APIs.
